questions.source_ref: Section 1.1: Motivation: From Non-Cooperative to Cooperative Settings
questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why mutual cooperation fails in the standard Prisoner's Dilemma, and what cooperative game theory assumes to overcome this? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Players cannot commit credibly to a joint course of action in the non-cooperative setting
Binding contracts are assumed to be feasible and enforceable in cooperative game theory
Mutual cooperation fails because players have no dominant strategy
Cooperative game theory abstracts away from the strategic details of how agreements are reached
Binding contracts allow players to rule out the non-cooperative outcome and implement mutually beneficial agreements

explanations.text: The key missing ingredient in the Prisoner's Dilemma is the ability to commit credibly to a joint course of action.
explanations.notes_quote: The key missing ingredient is the ability to commit credibly to a joint course of action.

explanations.text: Cooperative game theory assumes players can write and enforce binding contracts specifying what each will do and how payoffs are shared.
explanations.notes_quote: If players could write and enforce binding contracts specifying what each will do and how payoffs are shared, they could rule out the non-cooperative outcome

explanations.text: In the Prisoner's Dilemma each player does have a dominant strategy (defect). The failure of cooperation is due to the inability to make binding commitments, not the absence of a dominant strategy.
Cooperative game theory explicitly abstracts away from the strategic details of how agreements are reached.
explanations.notes_quote: Cooperative game theory abstracts away from the strategic details of how agreements are reached

explanations.text: With binding contracts, players can rule out the non-cooperative outcome and implement mutually beneficial agreements.
explanations.notes_quote: they could rule out the non-cooperative outcome and implement mutually beneficial agreements

questions.text: Which of the following are the three central questions asked by cooperative game theory? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Which coalitions can form?
What total value can each coalition generate?
How should value be divided under fairness and stability concepts such as the core and Shapley value?
What is the Nash equilibrium of the underlying stage game?
Which dominant strategies exist for each individual agent?

explanations.text: The first central question is which coalitions can form.
explanations.notes_quote: Which coalitions can form?

explanations.text: The second central question is what total value each coalition can generate.
explanations.notes_quote: What total value can they generate?

explanations.text: The third central question is how value should be divided under fairness and stability concepts.
explanations.notes_quote: How should this value be divided among participants under various fairness and stability concepts (e.g., the core, the Shapley value)?

explanations.text: Nash equilibrium is a non-cooperative concept. Cooperative game theory abstracts away from strategic details and focuses on coalition formation and payoff division.
Dominant strategies belong to non-cooperative game theory. Cooperative game theory focuses on what coalitions form and how value is divided, not individual strategic dominance.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish cooperative from non-cooperative game theory? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Non-cooperative games ask: what will each agent do independently?
Binding agreements are feasible and enforceable in cooperative games
Defection is always a concern in cooperative games, just as in non-cooperative games
Cooperative game theory focuses on collective actions and agreements of groups
In cooperative games, defection is eliminated by binding commitment

explanations.text: The comparison table gives the non-cooperative central question as: what will each agent do independently?
explanations.notes_quote: What will each agent do independently?

explanations.text: Binding agreements are listed as feasible and enforceable in the cooperative column.
explanations.notes_quote: Binding agreements: Feasible and enforceable

explanations.text: Defection is always a concern in non-cooperative games but is eliminated by binding commitment in cooperative games.
The cooperative focus is on collective actions and agreements of groups.
explanations.notes_quote: Focus: Collective actions and agreements of groups

explanations.text: In cooperative games, defection is eliminated by binding commitment.
explanations.notes_quote: Defection: Eliminated by binding commitment

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Transferable Utility (TU) games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A coalition generates a total pot of payoff that is then freely divided among its members
TU games are natural in monetary contexts where side payments are feasible
Individual payoffs are determined directly by the coalition's actions with no further redistribution
Partners splitting revenue is an example of a TU setting
TU games require all coalition members to receive equal shares of the total value

explanations.text: In TU games a coalition first generates a total pot of payoff and members then negotiate how to divide it.
explanations.notes_quote: a coalition first generates a total pot of payoff (joint profit or collective surplus), and then its members negotiate and agree on how to divide it among themselves

explanations.text: The divisibility assumption is natural in monetary contexts where side payments are feasible.
explanations.notes_quote: This divisibility assumption is natural in monetary contexts where side payments are feasible

explanations.text: Direct determination of payoffs with no redistribution describes NTU games. In TU games there is a separate division stage.
Partners splitting revenue is listed as an example of a TU monetary context.
explanations.notes_quote: Partners splitting revenue

explanations.text: TU games allow free redistribution of value; equal shares are not required. Members can divide the value in any agreed proportion.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Non-Transferable Utility (NTU) games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Individual payoffs cannot be freely redistributed among coalition members
The academic collaboration example (researchers rewarded by their home institution) is an NTU game
In NTU games, a coalition first generates a divisible pot that members then negotiate to split
Rewards in NTU games are often institution-specific and non-transferable
NTU games require a separate payoff division stage after value generation

explanations.text: In NTU games, individual payoffs are determined directly by actions and cannot be freely redistributed.
explanations.notes_quote: individual payoffs are determined directly by the coalition's actions and cannot be freely redistributed

explanations.text: The academic collaboration example is the NTU example in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Researchers across universities form collaboration groups. Each author is rewarded by their home institution... These rewards are institution-specific and non-transferable

explanations.text: Generating a divisible pot and then splitting it describes TU games, not NTU games.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state that rewards in the NTU academic example are institution-specific and non-transferable.
explanations.notes_quote: These rewards are institution-specific and non-transferable

explanations.text: A separate payoff division stage is a feature of TU games. In NTU games there is no redistribution.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game

questions.source_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the phases of a TU coalitional game and how they differ in NTU games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Coalition Formation Stage is where agents decide which coalitions to form, partitioning the player set
In TU games, generating value and receiving utility are two distinct stages
The Action Stage precedes the Coalition Formation Stage
In NTU games, the actions of all coalitions jointly determine each player's payoff directly, with no further redistribution
The Payoff Division Stage is where questions of fairness, stability, and individual incentives become acute in TU games

explanations.text: The first stage is Coalition Formation, where agents decide which coalitions to form.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game
explanations.notes_quote: Coalition Formation Stage: Agents decide which coalitions to form (partition of the player set into groups)

explanations.text: In TU games, generating value and receiving utility are two different things and require a division stage.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game
explanations.notes_quote: In TU games, generating value and receiving utility are two different things -- the division stage is required.

explanations.text: The Action Stage follows the Coalition Formation Stage. The correct order is: Coalition Formation → Action → Value Generation → Payoff Division.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game

explanations.text: In NTU games, all coalitions' actions jointly determine each player's payoff directly with no further redistribution.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game
explanations.notes_quote: In NTU games, the actions taken by all coalitions jointly determine each player's payoff directly with no further redistribution.

explanations.text: The Payoff Division Stage is where fairness, stability, and individual incentive questions become acute.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.3: Phases of a Coalitional Game
explanations.notes_quote: Members negotiate and agree on a payoff division -- this is where questions of fairness, stability, and individual incentives become acute

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the formal definition and standard assumptions of a TU characteristic function game $(N, v)$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $v: 2^N \to \mathbb{R}$ assigns a real value to each coalition $C \subseteq N$
The set $N$ of all players is called the grand coalition
Standard assumptions include normalisation ($v(\emptyset) = 0$) and non-negativity ($v(C) \geq 0$)
Monotonicity requires: if $S \subseteq T$ then $v(S) \geq v(T)$
$v(C)$ represents the amount coalition $C$ can earn by working together

explanations.text: The characteristic function maps each coalition to a real value.
explanations.notes_quote: $v: 2^N \to \mathbb{R}$ is the characteristic function that assigns to each coalition $C \subseteq N$ a real value $v(C)$

explanations.text: The set $N$ of all players is defined as the grand coalition.
explanations.notes_quote: The set $N$ of all players is called the grand coalition.

explanations.text: Both normalisation and non-negativity are standard assumptions.
explanations.notes_quote: Normalized: $v(\emptyset) = 0$; Non-negative: $v(C) \geq 0$ for all $C \subseteq N$

explanations.text: Monotonicity requires $v(S) \leq v(T)$ when $S \subseteq T$ — the inequality is reversed. Larger coalitions have at least as much value.
$v(C)$ is the amount coalition members can earn by working together.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C)$ is the amount that the members of $C$ can earn by working together (taking their best possible action)

questions.source_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games
questions.text: Which of the following correctly distinguish Partition Function Games (PFGs) from Characteristic Function Games (CFGs)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In a PFG, a coalition's payoff depends on both its own composition and the partition of remaining players
In a CFG, a coalition's payoff is independent of the actions and arrangement of outsiders
The core and Shapley value were originally developed for PFGs because their richer structure enables tractable analysis
The Ice Cream Game is an example of a CFG
The Farmers' game (where market prices respond to total supply from all coalitions) is an example of a PFG

explanations.text: In a PFG, payoff depends on the coalition's composition AND the partition of remaining players.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games
explanations.notes_quote: Partition Function Game (PFG): Payoff depends on the coalition's own composition AND the partition of remaining players and their actions

explanations.text: In a CFG, payoff depends only on the coalition's own composition and choice, independent of outsiders.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games
explanations.notes_quote: Characteristic Function Game (CFG): Payoff depends only on the coalition's own composition and choice (independent of outsiders)

explanations.text: The core and Shapley value were developed for CFGs, not PFGs. CFGs' simpler payoff structure makes analysis tractable. Extending to PFGs is an active research frontier.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games

explanations.text: The Ice Cream Game is listed as the CFG example.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games
explanations.notes_quote: Characteristic Function Game (CFG): ... Ice cream game

explanations.text: The Farmers' game is a PFG because market prices respond to total supply from all coalitions.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.2: Partition Function Games vs. Characteristic Function Games
explanations.notes_quote: Farmers' game (market prices respond to total supply from all coalitions)

questions.text: In the Ice Cream Game (Charlie: £4, Marcy: £3, Patty: £2; tubs cost £9 for 1 kg, £7 for 750 g, £5 for 500 g), which of the following coalition values are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $v(\{C\}) = 0$ because £4 is less than the cheapest tub priced at £5
$v(\{C, M\}) = 750$ because together they pool £7 to buy the 750 g tub
$v(\{M, P\}) = 750$ because together they pool £5
$v(\{C, M, P\}) = 1000$ because they pool £9 to buy the 1 kg tub
$v(\{C, P\}) = 500$ because together they pool £6 to buy the 500 g tub

explanations.text: Charlie alone has £4, less than the cheapest tub (£5), so $v(\{C\}) = 0$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\{C\}$: Money = 4, Can buy = Nothing (cheapest is 5), $v(C) = 0$

explanations.text: Charlie + Marcy = £7, which buys the 750 g tub, so $v(\{C,M\}) = 750$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\{C, M\}$: Money = 7, Can buy = 750g tub, $v(C) = 750$

explanations.text: Marcy + Patty pool only £5, which buys the 500 g tub (not 750 g), so $v(\{M,P\}) = 500$, not 750.
explanations.notes_quote: $\{M, P\}$: Money = 5, Can buy = 500g tub, $v(C) = 500$

explanations.text: All three pool £9, buying the 1 kg (1000 g) tub, so $v(\{C,M,P\}) = 1000$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\{C, M, P\}$: Money = 9, Can buy = 1kg tub, $v(C) = 1000$

explanations.text: Charlie + Patty pool £6, buying the 500 g tub, so $v(\{C,P\}) = 500$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\{C, P\}$: Money = 6, Can buy = 500g tub, $v(C) = 500$

questions.source_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a valid outcome $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ of a TU game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $CS$ is a partition of $N$ into disjoint coalitions whose union is $N$
The payoff vector must satisfy $\sum_{i \in C_j} x_i = v(C_j)$ for each coalition $C_j \in CS$
Players may receive negative payoffs in a valid outcome
The notation $x(C) = \sum_{i \in C} x_i$ is used for convenience to denote the sum of payoffs in coalition $C$
All singletons $\{\{C\},\{M\},\{P\}\}$ with $\mathbf{x}=(0,0,0)$ is a valid outcome of the Ice Cream game

explanations.text: $CS$ is defined as a partition of $N$ into disjoint coalitions whose union is $N$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game
explanations.notes_quote: $CS = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ is a coalition structure -- a partition of $N$ into disjoint coalitions whose union is $N$

explanations.text: Each coalition must distribute exactly its characteristic function value.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game
explanations.notes_quote: For each coalition $C_j \in CS$: $\sum_{i \in C_j} x_i = v(C_j)$ (the coalition distributes exactly its value)

explanations.text: A valid outcome requires $x_i \geq 0$ for each player $i$. Negative payoffs are not permitted.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game

explanations.text: The notation $x(C) = \sum_{i \in C} x_i$ is introduced for convenience.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game
explanations.notes_quote: We write $x(C) = \sum_{i \in C} x_i$ for convenience.

explanations.text: All singletons with $\mathbf{x}=(0,0,0)$ is listed as a valid outcome: each singleton has $v=0$ and distributes 0.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.4: Outcomes of a TU Game
explanations.notes_quote: All singletons: $CS = \{\{C\}, \{M\}, \{P\}\}$, $\mathbf{x} = (0, 0, 0)$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe imputations in a TU game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An imputation requires individual rationality: $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$ for all $i \in N$
Individual rationality means each agent receives at least as much as they would earn by forming a singleton coalition
Every valid outcome $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ automatically qualifies as an imputation
A valid outcome can fail to be an imputation if some player receives less than their singleton value
Imputations guarantee that the coalition structure maximises social welfare

explanations.text: An imputation requires $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$ for all $i \in N$ (individual rationality).
explanations.notes_quote: $x_i \geq v(\{i\}) \quad \text{for all } i \in N$

explanations.text: Individual rationality ensures each agent does at least as well as their singleton payoff.
explanations.notes_quote: Each agent receives at least as much as they would earn by forming a singleton coalition.

explanations.text: A valid outcome only requires $x_i \geq 0$ and exact distribution of coalition value. An imputation additionally requires $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$, a stricter condition.
If any player $i$ receives $x_i < v(\{i\})$, the outcome fails individual rationality and is not an imputation.
explanations.notes_quote: $x_i \geq v(\{i\}) \quad \text{for all } i \in N$

explanations.text: Maximising social welfare (efficiency) is a property of core outcomes, not imputations. Imputations only enforce individual rationality.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the structure of weighted voting games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each player has a weight $w_i$ and there is a quota $q$
$v(C) = 1$ if $w(C) = \sum_{i \in C} w_i \geq q$
A winning coalition receives value equal to its total weight $w(C)$
Weighted voting games with non-negative weights are simple games

explanations.text: A weighted voting game is defined by $N$ players each with weight $w_i$ and a quota $q$.
explanations.notes_quote: $N$ players, each with a weight $w_i$; A quota $q$

explanations.text: $v(C) = 1$ when the coalition's total weight meets the quota.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C) = 1$ if $w(C) \geq q$ (winning coalition)

explanations.text: $v(C) = 0$ when the coalition's total weight falls below the quota.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C) = 0$ if $w(C) < q$ (losing coalition)

explanations.text: Weighted voting games are simple games: a winning coalition gets value 1, not its total weight. The characteristic function is binary.
Weighted voting games with non-negative weights are simple games.
explanations.notes_quote: Weighted voting games with non-negative weights are simple games.

questions.text: In the UK General Election 2010 (quota = 326; Conservative: 306, Labour: 258, Lib Dem: 57, SNP: 6, DUP: 8), which of the following coalition evaluations are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Conservative alone formed a winning coalition in 2010

explanations.text: Conservative + Labour = 564 seats $\geq$ 326, so $v(\{Con, Lab\}) = 1$.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(\{Con, Lab\}) = 1$ (564 seats)

explanations.text: Conservative + Lib Dem = 363 seats $\geq$ 326, so $v(\{Con, LD\}) = 1$.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(\{Con, LD\}) = 1$ (363 seats)

explanations.text: Labour + Lib Dem = 315 seats $< 326$, so $v(\{Lab, LD\}) = 0$ (a losing coalition).
Labour + Lib Dem + SNP + DUP = 329 $\geq$ 326, forming a winning coalition.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(\{Lab, LD, SNP, DUP\}) = 1$ (329 seats)

explanations.text: In 2010 Conservative had only 306 seats, below the 326 quota. Conservative alone became a winning coalition in 2019 (365 seats), not 2010.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Network Flow Games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Players are the edges of a network with source $s$, sink $t$, and edge capacities
$v(C)$ is the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ using only the edges in coalition $C$
Players are the vertices of the network
Network flow games are superadditive
$v(C)$ is the total weight of edges internal to the subgraph induced by $C$

explanations.text: In network flow games, players are the edges of the network.
explanations.notes_quote: Network $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$, sink $t$, and edge capacities; Players are the edges

explanations.text: $v(C)$ equals the maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ using only edges in coalition $C$.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C)$ = maximum flow from $s$ to $t$ using only the edges in coalition $C$

explanations.text: Players are edges in network flow games. Players as vertices is the setup for graph-induced games.
Network flow games are listed as the canonical example of superadditive games.
explanations.notes_quote: Example: Network flow games are superadditive.

explanations.text: Total internal edge weight is the value function for graph-induced games, not network flow games.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Graph-Induced Games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Players are vertices of a weighted graph
$v(C)$ is the total weight of edges internal to the subgraph induced by $C$
Edge weights must be strictly positive to model social networks
An example outcome gives each player half the weight of their adjacent edges
$v(C)$ equals the maximum flow through the subgraph induced by $C$

explanations.text: In graph-induced games, players are vertices of a weighted graph.
explanations.notes_quote: Players are vertices of a weighted graph

explanations.text: $v(C)$ is the total weight of edges internal to the subgraph induced by $C$.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C)$ = total weight of edges internal to the subgraph induced by $C$

explanations.text: Edge weights can be negative (representing negative relationships). The notes explicitly mention this possibility.
explanations.notes_quote: edge weights can be negative (negative relationships)

explanations.text: The notes give as an example outcome the grand coalition where each player receives half the weight of their adjacent edges.
explanations.notes_quote: Example outcome: grand coalition where each player receives half the weight of their adjacent edges

explanations.text: Maximum flow is the value function for network flow games. Graph-induced games use total internal edge weight.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe simple games and player types within them? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A game is simple if $v(C) \in \{0, 1\}$ for all $C \subseteq N$ and the characteristic function is monotone
A null player is one such that $v(C \cup \{i\}) = v(C)$ for all $C$ — adding them never changes coalition value
A veto player is one such that $v(C) = 0$ whenever $i \notin C$ — no coalition can win without them
In simple games, players are assumed to form all possible two-player coalitions rather than the grand coalition
Due to monotonicity, players in simple games are typically assumed to form the grand coalition

explanations.text: A simple game requires binary values and monotonicity.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(C) \in \{0, 1\}$ for all $C \subseteq N$; The characteristic function is monotone

explanations.text: A null player's addition to any coalition leaves the value unchanged.
explanations.notes_quote: Null player: $v(C \cup \{i\}) = v(C)$ for all $C$ -- Adding player $i$ to any coalition does not change its value

explanations.text: A veto player is one whose absence makes every coalition lose.
explanations.notes_quote: Veto player: $v(C) = 0$ whenever $i \notin C$ -- No coalition can win without player $i$

explanations.text: In simple games players are assumed to form the grand coalition, not all two-player coalitions, because monotonicity means larger coalitions are at least as good.
Due to monotonicity, in simple games players are typically assumed to form the grand coalition.
explanations.notes_quote: In simple games, due to monotonicity, we typically assume players form the grand coalition (unless specified otherwise).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the UK election examples for null and veto players? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In the 2010 election there were no null players and no veto players
In the 2019 election (Conservative: 365), Conservative alone was a winning coalition, making it a veto player
In the 2019 election all non-Conservative parties became null players
In the 2017 election (Conservative: 317) Conservative was a veto player because no other party could form a majority without them
In the 2010 election Conservative paired with any other listed party formed a winning coalition

explanations.text: The notes state that in 2010 there were no null players and no veto players.
explanations.notes_quote: 2010: No null players, no veto players

explanations.text: In 2019 Conservative had 365 seats $\geq$ 326, forming a winning coalition alone and becoming a veto player.
explanations.notes_quote: 2019 (Con: 365): Conservative alone was a winning coalition ($365 \geq 326$), making it a veto player

explanations.text: When one player is a veto player with a solo majority, all others are null players.
explanations.notes_quote: making it a veto player and all other players null players

explanations.text: In 2017 Conservative was NOT a veto player — other parties together could still form a winning coalition without them: $v(N \setminus \{Con\}) = 1$.
explanations.notes_quote: 2017 (Con: 317): Conservative was not a veto player (others together could still win without them: $v(N \setminus \{Con\}) = 1$)

explanations.text: In 2010 Conservative paired with any other listed party formed a winning coalition.
explanations.notes_quote: Conservative paired with any other party formed a winning coalition

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe superadditive games and their implications? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A game is superadditive if $v(S \cup T) \geq v(S) + v(T)$ for all disjoint $S, T \subseteq N$
Superadditivity implies players always have incentives to merge, so the grand coalition can be assumed
In superadditive games, outcomes can be identified with just the payoff vector $\mathbf{x}$ without specifying the coalition structure
Superadditivity implies convexity
Network flow games are superadditive

explanations.text: The formal definition of superadditivity requires the merged coalition's value to be at least the sum of the parts'.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(S \cup T) \geq v(S) + v(T) \quad \text{for all disjoint } S, T \subseteq N$

explanations.text: Superadditivity means players always have incentives to merge, so the grand coalition can be assumed.
explanations.notes_quote: Players always have incentives to merge, so we can assume they form the grand coalition.

explanations.text: In superadditive games, since the grand coalition forms, outcomes need only specify the payoff vector.
explanations.notes_quote: outcomes can be identified with just the payoff vector $\mathbf{x}$ (no need to specify coalition structure)

explanations.text: The relationship is the reverse: convexity (supermodularity) implies superadditivity, but not the other way around.
explanations.notes_quote: Supermodular $\Rightarrow$ Superadditive (but not conversely)

explanations.text: Network flow games are listed as the canonical example of superadditive games.
explanations.notes_quote: Example: Network flow games are superadditive.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe convex games and the supermodularity condition? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A TU game is convex if its characteristic function is supermodular
Supermodularity requires $f(A \cup B) + f(A \cap B) \geq f(A) + f(B)$ for any $A, B \subseteq N$
In a convex game, a player is more useful when joining a bigger coalition
Formally, convexity means: if $T \subseteq S$ and $i \notin S$, then $v(T \cup \{i\}) - v(T) \leq v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)$
All superadditive games are also convex

explanations.text: A TU game is convex if its characteristic function is supermodular.
explanations.notes_quote: A TU game is convex if its characteristic function is supermodular.

explanations.text: Supermodularity is defined by the inequality $f(A \cup B) + f(A \cap B) \geq f(A) + f(B)$.
explanations.notes_quote: For any $A, B \subseteq N$ (not necessarily disjoint): $f(A \cup B) + f(A \cap B) \geq f(A) + f(B)$

explanations.text: The key property of convex games is that a player is more useful when joining a bigger coalition.
explanations.notes_quote: Key property of convex games: A player is more useful when joining a bigger coalition.

explanations.text: This is the formal statement of increasing marginal contributions in convex games.
explanations.notes_quote: if $T \subseteq S$ and $i \notin S$, then $v(T \cup \{i\}) - v(T) \leq v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)$

explanations.text: Supermodularity (convexity) implies superadditivity, but not vice versa — the ice cream game is superadditive but not convex.
explanations.notes_quote: Supermodular $\Rightarrow$ Superadditive (but not conversely)

questions.text: Regarding the properties of the three example games (ice cream, graph-induced, weighted voting), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The ice cream game is superadditive but not convex
The graph-induced game is superadditive and convex if and only if all edge weights are non-negative
A weighted voting game with $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$ is convex
A weighted voting game is superadditive only if $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$
The three-player majority game ($w_1=w_2=w_3=1$, $q=2$) is not convex, shown by $A=\{1,2\}$, $B=\{2,3\}$: $v(A\cup B)+v(A\cap B)=1 < v(A)+v(B)=2$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly classify the ice cream game as superadditive but not convex.
explanations.notes_quote: Ice cream game: Superadditive? Yes; Convex? No

explanations.text: Graph-induced games are superadditive and convex if and only if all edge weights are non-negative.
explanations.notes_quote: Graph-induced game: Superadditive? Iff all edge weights $\geq 0$; Convex? Iff all edge weights $\geq 0$

explanations.text: A weighted voting game with $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$ is superadditive but not convex — the notes explicitly state it is not convex even in this case.
explanations.notes_quote: Weighted voting game: Convex? Not even when $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$

explanations.text: Weighted voting games are superadditive only if $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$.
explanations.notes_quote: Weighted voting game: Superadditive? Only if $q > \frac{w(N)}{2}$

explanations.text: The counterexample: $v(A\cup B)+v(A\cap B)=1+0=1 < v(A)+v(B)=1+1=2$, violating supermodularity.
explanations.notes_quote: $v(A \cup B) + v(A \cap B) = v(\{1,2,3\}) + v(\{2\}) = 1 + 0 = 1$, but $v(A) + v(B) = 1 + 1 = 2$. Since $1 < 2$, this is not convex.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the two criteria for evaluating outcomes in cooperative games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Stability asks whether any group has an incentive to deviate from the coalition structure
Fairness asks how well each agent's payoff reflects their contribution
The core is a fairness-based solution concept
The Shapley value is a stability-based solution concept
Stability and fairness give rise to two distinct families of solution concepts

explanations.text: Stability asks what incentives agents have to stay in the coalition structure — no group should want to deviate.
explanations.notes_quote: Stability: What incentives do agents have to stay in the coalition structure? (No group should want to deviate.)

explanations.text: Fairness asks how well each agent's payoff reflects their contribution.
explanations.notes_quote: Fairness: How well does each agent's payoff reflect their contribution?

explanations.text: The core is a stability-based concept. The Shapley value is the fairness-based concept.
The Shapley value is a fairness-based concept, not stability-based. The core is the stability concept.
Stability and fairness give rise to two families of solution concepts.
explanations.notes_quote: These give rise to two families of solution concepts.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the core $\mathcal{C}(G)$ of a characteristic function game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The core is the set of all outcomes $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ such that $x(C) \geq v(C)$ for all $C \subseteq N$
The core condition ensures no coalition $C$ can deviate and do better on its own
A coalition wants to deviate if its members collectively receive strictly less than $v(C)$ in the current outcome
The core condition $x(C) \geq v(C)$ must hold only for the grand coalition, not for proper subsets
The core may be empty for some games

explanations.text: The core is formally defined as the set of outcomes where $x(C) \geq v(C)$ for all $C \subseteq N$.
explanations.notes_quote: The core $\mathcal{C}(G)$ ... is the set of all outcomes $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ such that $x(C) \geq v(C) \quad \text{for all } C \subseteq N$

explanations.text: The core ensures no coalition can deviate because members already collectively receive at least $v(C)$.
explanations.notes_quote: No coalition $C$ can deviate and do better on its own, because the members of $C$ already collectively receive at least $v(C)$ in the current outcome.

explanations.text: A coalition wants to deviate when its collective payoff is less than $v(C)$ — it can guarantee itself more by acting independently.
explanations.notes_quote: No coalition $C$ can deviate and do better on its own, because the members of $C$ already collectively receive at least $v(C)$

explanations.text: The core condition must hold for ALL coalitions $C \subseteq N$, including all proper subsets.
explanations.notes_quote: $x(C) \geq v(C) \quad \text{for all } C \subseteq N$

explanations.text: Some games have empty cores — no stable outcome exists.
explanations.notes_quote: some games have empty cores -- no stable outcome exists

questions.source_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
questions.text: Which of the following are valid core outcomes of the Ice Cream Game ($v(\{C,M,P\})=1000$, $v(\{C,M\})=750$, $v(\{C,P\})=500$, $v(\{M,P\})=500$)? (Select ALL that apply.)

explanations.text: $(400,400,200)$: sums to 1000; $x_C+x_M=800\geq750$; $x_C+x_P=600\geq500$; $x_M+x_P=600\geq500$. All core constraints satisfied.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: Some core outcomes: $(400, 400, 200)$

explanations.text: $(500,400,100)$: sums to 1000; $x_C+x_M=900\geq750$; $x_C+x_P=600\geq500$; $x_M+x_P=500\geq500$. All constraints satisfied.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: Some core outcomes: $(500, 400, 100)$

explanations.text: $(350,350,300)$ is NOT in the core: $x_C+x_M=700 < 750$, so Charlie and Marcy would deviate.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: $(350, 350, 300)$ is NOT in the core because $x_C + x_M = 700 < 750$, so Charlie and Marcy would deviate.

explanations.text: $(500,500,0)$: sums to 1000; $x_C+x_M=1000\geq750$; $x_C+x_P=500\geq500$; $x_M+x_P=500\geq500$. All constraints satisfied.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: Some core outcomes: $(500, 500, 0)$

explanations.text: $(0,800,200)$: $x_C+x_P=200<500$, so Charlie and Patty would deviate. Not in the core.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game

questions.source_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
questions.text: Which of the following are correct about the coalition structure and constraints in any core outcome of the Ice Cream Game? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Any core outcome must use the grand coalition $\{C,M,P\}$ as the coalition structure
The grand coalition must form because $v(\{C,M,P\})=1000$ exceeds the social welfare of any other partition
The partition $\{\{C,M\},\{P\}\}$ could be a valid core coalition structure
Core outcomes require $x_C + x_M + x_P = 1000$
Core outcomes require $x_C + x_M \geq 750$

explanations.text: Any core outcome uses the grand coalition because no other partition achieves the same social welfare.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: For any outcome in the core, the coalition structure must be the grand coalition

explanations.text: The grand coalition generates value 1000, which exceeds the sum of values under any other partition.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: the coalition structure must be the grand coalition (because $v(\{C,M,P\}) = 1000$ exceeds the social welfare of any other partition)

explanations.text: The partition $\{\{C,M\},\{P\}\}$ generates only $750+0=750 < 1000$. Core outcomes require the grand coalition.
The grand coalition distributes its full value of 1000.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game

explanations.text: The core constraint for coalition $\{C,M\}$ requires $x_C+x_M \geq v(\{C,M\}) = 750$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 6.3: Core of the Ice Cream Game
explanations.notes_quote: $x_C + x_M \geq 750$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state or follow from the theorem that stability implies efficiency? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ is in the core of a CFG, then $CS$ maximises social welfare over all possible coalition structures
The proof proceeds by contradiction: assume $CS$ is in the core but a better coalition structure $CS'$ exists
The contradiction arises because the core condition implies $x(C') \geq v(C')$ for every $C' \in CS'$, but the assumption requires the opposite
A core outcome can be stable but inefficient — efficiency is a separate property
Core outcomes always maximise the total payoff distributed to all players

explanations.text: Core outcomes always maximise social welfare over all possible coalition structures.
explanations.notes_quote: If an outcome $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ is in the core of a CFG $G$, then $CS$ maximises social welfare (the total payoff to all players) over all possible coalition structures.

explanations.text: The proof assumes a core outcome $CS$ alongside a better coalition structure $CS'$ and derives a contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: Suppose $CS$ is in the core but there exists $CS'$ with $\text{val}(CS') > \text{val}(CS)$.

explanations.text: The core condition forces $x(C') \geq v(C')$ for every $C' \in CS'$, contradicting the assumption that $\sum v(C') > \sum x(C')$.
explanations.notes_quote: since $(CS, \mathbf{x})$ is in the core, $x(C') \geq v(C')$ for every $C' \in CS'$

explanations.text: The theorem proves the opposite: stability automatically implies efficiency. They are not independent.
explanations.notes_quote: Therefore, core outcomes are always efficient.

explanations.text: Core outcomes maximise total payoff (social welfare) — the definition of efficiency here.
explanations.notes_quote: core outcomes are always efficient

questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why the three-player majority game ($w_1=w_2=w_3=1$, $q=2$) has an empty core? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Under three singletons, all payoffs are 0 but the grand coalition can deviate and get value 1
Under a pair-and-singleton structure, the disadvantaged player in the pair can team with the singleton to form a new winning coalition
Under the grand coalition, some player $i$ has $x_i > 0$, and $N \setminus \{i\}$ can deviate because $x(N\setminus\{i\}) < v(N\setminus\{i\})$
The core is empty because no player satisfies individual rationality
The core is empty because in every case some coalition has an incentive to deviate

explanations.text: With three singletons all payoffs are 0, but the grand coalition can deviate and secure value 1.
explanations.notes_quote: Three singletons: All payoffs must be 0, but the grand coalition can deviate and win (getting value 1).

explanations.text: In the pair-and-singleton case, the lower-paid member can defect to form a new winning pair with the singleton.
explanations.notes_quote: the disadvantaged player teams with player 3 to form a winning coalition and both get more

explanations.text: Under the grand coalition, some player $i$ must have $x_i > 0$; then $x(N\setminus\{i\}) < 1 = v(N\setminus\{i\})$, so that pair can deviate.
explanations.notes_quote: Some player $i$ has $x_i > 0$... The coalition $N \setminus \{i\}$ has $x(N \setminus \{i\}) < 1 = v(N \setminus \{i\})$, so that pair can deviate.

explanations.text: Individual rationality is not the issue — with $v(\{i\})=0$ any non-negative payoff satisfies it. The core is empty because of coalition deviations.
The proof by case analysis shows that in every possible coalition structure some coalition has an incentive to deviate.
explanations.notes_quote: In every case, some coalition has an incentive to deviate, so the core is empty.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the motivation for the Shapley value? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The core captures stability but does not guarantee fairness
$(500, 500, 0)$ is in the core of the ice cream game, but giving Patty nothing seems unfair
The Shapley value is based on the intuition that each agent's payment should be proportional to their contribution
The Shapley value was designed to replace the core wherever the core is non-empty
The Shapley value is a stability-based solution concept like the core

explanations.text: The core captures stability but a core outcome can be unfair.
explanations.notes_quote: The core captures stability, but is it fair?

explanations.text: $(500,500,0)$ is a valid core outcome, yet giving Patty nothing is cited as the motivating unfairness.
explanations.notes_quote: $(500, 500, 0)$ is in the core of the ice cream game, but giving Patty nothing seems unfair.

explanations.text: The Shapley value is based on paying each agent proportional to their contribution.
explanations.notes_quote: each agent's payment should be proportional to their contribution to the coalitions they participate in

explanations.text: The Shapley value addresses fairness and is always well-defined; it is not designed to replace the core. Both coexist.
The Shapley value is a fairness-based concept, not stability-based.
explanations.notes_quote: Shapley value: Fairness — Average marginal contribution over all orderings

questions.source_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?
questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why paying each player their marginal contribution to the grand coalition fails? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The total payments $\sum_i [v(N) - v(N \setminus \{i\})]$ may not equal $v(N)$
In the three-player majority game, each player's marginal contribution to $N$ is $1-1=0$, so $\sum_i x_i = 0 \neq 1$
This approach violates individual rationality because players can earn more as singletons
The naive approach is efficient only in superadditive games
The naive marginal contribution approach fails the efficiency property

explanations.text: The sum of marginal contributions to the grand coalition need not equal $v(N)$, violating efficiency.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?
explanations.notes_quote: The total payoff $\sum_i x_i$ may not equal $v(N)$.

explanations.text: In the three-player majority game $v(N)-v(N\setminus\{i\})=1-1=0$ for each player, giving total payments of 0 instead of 1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?
explanations.notes_quote: In the three-player majority game, $v(N) - v(N \setminus \{i\}) = 1 - 1 = 0$ for each player, so $\sum_i x_i = 0 \neq 1 = v(N)$.

explanations.text: Individual rationality is not the issue. The problem is the failure of efficiency (payments not summing to $v(N)$).
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?

explanations.text: The notes do not claim this. The three-player majority game (superadditive) is the counterexample.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?

explanations.text: The naive approach fails because $\sum_i x_i$ may not equal $v(N)$, which is the efficiency requirement.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?
explanations.notes_quote: The total payoff $\sum_i x_i$ may not equal $v(N)$.

questions.source_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the fixed-ordering approach and its shortcomings? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In a fixed ordering, agent $k$ is paid $v(\{1,\ldots,k\}) - v(\{1,\ldots,k-1\})$
The fixed-ordering approach is efficient — payments sum to $v(N)$
The fixed-ordering approach is fair because symmetric players always receive the same payoff
In the three-player majority game with ordering $(1,2,3)$: agent 1 gets 0, agent 2 gets 1, agent 3 gets 0
The fixed-ordering approach is rejected because it is both inefficient and unfair

explanations.text: Each agent receives their marginal contribution to the coalition of predecessors.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
explanations.notes_quote: Agent $k$ gets $v(\{1,\ldots,k\}) - v(\{1,\ldots,k-1\})$

explanations.text: The fixed-ordering approach is efficient: payments telescope and sum to $v(N)$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
explanations.notes_quote: This is efficient (sums to $v(N)$)

explanations.text: The fixed-ordering approach is unfair: symmetric players may receive very different payoffs depending on the ordering.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
explanations.notes_quote: unfair: symmetric players may receive very different payoffs depending on the ordering

explanations.text: With ordering $(1,2,3)$: agent 1 gets 0, agent 2 gets 1, agent 3 gets 0. But all three players are identical.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
explanations.notes_quote: agent 1 gets 0, agent 2 gets 1 (becomes a winning pair), agent 3 gets 0. But all three players are identical.

explanations.text: The fixed-ordering approach IS efficient. It is rejected because it is unfair, not inefficient.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.3: Why Not a Fixed Ordering?
explanations.notes_quote: This is efficient (sums to $v(N)$), but unfair

questions.source_ref: Section 7.4: The Shapley Value: Averaging Over All Orderings
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Shapley value formula and its probabilistic interpretation? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Shapley value averages marginal contributions over all $n!$ permutations of $N$
$S^{\pi}(i) = \{j \in N : \pi(j) < \pi(i)\}$ denotes the set of predecessors of $i$ in permutation $\pi$
$\phi_i(v) = \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\pi \in \Pi(N)} [v(S^{\pi}(i) \cup \{i\}) - v(S^{\pi}(i))]$
$\phi_i(v)$ is the expected marginal contribution of player $i$ when a permutation is chosen uniformly at random
The Shapley value averages over all $2^{n-1}$ coalitions not containing $i$

explanations.text: The Shapley value averages over all $n!$ permutations.
explanations.notes_quote: average marginal contributions over all permutations

explanations.text: $S^{\pi}(i)$ is defined as the set of predecessors of $i$ in permutation $\pi$.
explanations.notes_quote: $S^{\pi}(i) = \{j \in N : \pi(j) < \pi(i)\}$ be the set of predecessors of $i$ in $\pi$

explanations.text: This is the exact formula for the Shapley value.
explanations.notes_quote: $\phi_i(v) = \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\pi \in \Pi(N)} \Delta_i^{\pi}(v)$

explanations.text: $\phi_i(v)$ is the expected marginal contribution when a permutation is chosen uniformly at random.
explanations.notes_quote: If we choose a permutation uniformly at random, $\phi_i(v)$ is the expected marginal contribution of player $i$ to the coalition of their predecessors.

explanations.text: Averaging over all $2^{n-1}$ coalitions not containing $i$ is the Banzhaf index, not the Shapley value.

questions.source_ref: Section 7.5: Computing the Shapley Value: Ice Cream Example (Patty)
questions.text: In the Ice Cream Game, which of the following are correct about Patty's Shapley value computation? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: When Patty's predecessors are $\{C, M\}$, her marginal contribution is $1000 - 750 = 250$
When Patty is first in the ordering (predecessors $= \emptyset$), her marginal contribution is $v(\{P\}) - v(\emptyset) = 0$
When Patty's only predecessor is Charlie, her marginal contribution is $v(\{C,P\}) - v(\{C\}) = 500 - 0 = 500$
Patty's Shapley value equals her marginal contribution to the grand coalition, $v(N) - v(N\setminus\{P\}) = 250$, which is always equal to the Shapley value

explanations.text: When predecessors are $\{C,M\}$, Patty's marginal contribution is $1000-750=250$.
When Patty is first, predecessors $=\emptyset$, marginal contribution $= v(\{P\}) - v(\emptyset) = 0$.
With only Charlie as predecessor, $v(\{C,P\})-v(\{C\})=500-0=500$.
Patty's Shapley value is the average of her six marginal contributions: $1500/6 = 250$.

The numerical coincidence here is not general. Section 7.2 shows the simple marginal contribution to $N$ need not equal the Shapley value — in the majority game all marginal contributions to $N$ are 0.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.2: Why Not Simple Marginal Contribution?

questions.source_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the four axiomatic properties of the Shapley value? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Efficiency: $\sum_{i \in N} \phi_i(v) = v(N)$
Null Player: if $v(C \cup \{i\}) = v(C)$ for all $C \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}$, then $\phi_i(v) = 0$
Symmetry: if $v(C \cup \{i\}) = v(C \cup \{j\})$ for all $C \subseteq N \setminus \{i,j\}$, then $\phi_i(v) = \phi_j(v)$
Additivity: $\phi_i(v_1 + v_2) = \phi_i(v_1) + \phi_i(v_2)$
Monotonicity: $\phi_i(v) \geq \phi_j(v)$ whenever player $i$ has a higher weight than player $j$

explanations.text: Efficiency requires the Shapley value to distribute the full value of the grand coalition.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: Efficiency: $\sum_{i \in N} \phi_i(v) = v(N)$. It distributes the full value of the grand coalition.

explanations.text: A null (dummy) player who contributes nothing to any coalition receives zero.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: Null (Dummy) Player: If $v(C \cup \{i\}) = v(C)$ for all $C \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}$, then $\phi_i(v) = 0$.

explanations.text: Symmetric players receive equal Shapley values.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: Symmetry: If players $i$ and $j$ are symmetric ... then $\phi_i(v) = \phi_j(v)$.

explanations.text: Additivity means the Shapley value of a sum of games equals the sum of individual Shapley values.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: Additivity: If $G^+ = (N, v_1 + v_2)$ ... then $\phi_i(v_1 + v_2) = \phi_i(v_1) + \phi_i(v_2)$.

explanations.text: Monotonicity is not among the four Shapley axioms. The four are efficiency, null player, symmetry, and additivity.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value

questions.source_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Uniqueness Theorem for the Shapley value? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Shapley value is the only payoff division scheme satisfying efficiency, null player, symmetry, and additivity
There exist other efficient and symmetric schemes that also satisfy null player and additivity
The Banzhaf index also satisfies all four Shapley axioms
Uniqueness means no other function can satisfy all four axioms simultaneously
Uniqueness means the Shapley value is the only fair allocation method in all of cooperative game theory

explanations.text: The Uniqueness Theorem states the Shapley value is the only scheme satisfying all four axioms.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: The Shapley value is the only payoff division scheme satisfying all four properties.

explanations.text: The Uniqueness Theorem proves no other scheme can satisfy all four axioms simultaneously.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value

explanations.text: The Banzhaf index fails efficiency, so it does not satisfy all four Shapley axioms.
explanations.notes_quote: Efficiency: No -- the Banzhaf index does NOT necessarily distribute $v(N)$

explanations.text: Uniqueness means exactly that no other function simultaneously satisfies all four axioms.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value
explanations.notes_quote: The Shapley value is the only payoff division scheme satisfying all four properties.

explanations.text: Uniqueness is relative to the four specific axioms. The Banzhaf index is another valid fairness measure with different properties.

questions.source_ref: Section 7.7: Using Properties to Simplify Computation
questions.text: For the game with $n$ players where $v(N)=1$ and $v(C)=0$ for all $C \subsetneq N$, which of the following correctly compute or justify the Shapley value? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: By direct computation: player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 only when last in the permutation, giving $\phi_i = \frac{(n-1)!}{n!} = \frac{1}{n}$
By symmetry and efficiency: all players are symmetric so each gets the same, and efficiency forces $\phi_i = \frac{1}{n}$
Player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 whenever $|S^{\pi}(i)| \geq 1$
There are $(n-1)!$ permutations where player $i$ is last out of $n!$ total
The null player axiom gives $\phi_i = 0$ for all players in this game

explanations.text: Player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 only in the $(n-1)!$ permutations where $i$ is last, giving $\phi_i = 1/n$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.7: Using Properties to Simplify Computation
explanations.notes_quote: Player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 only if $i$ is last in the permutation ($(n-1)!$ such permutations out of $n!$). So $\phi_i = \frac{(n-1)!}{n!} = \frac{1}{n}$.

explanations.text: Symmetry and efficiency together immediately give $\phi_i = 1/n$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.7: Using Properties to Simplify Computation
explanations.notes_quote: All players are symmetric, so by symmetry they all get the same value. By efficiency, $n \cdot \phi_i = 1$, so $\phi_i = \frac{1}{n}$.

explanations.text: Player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 only when ALL other $n-1$ players are predecessors (i.e., $i$ is last). With fewer predecessors, $v(S^{\pi}(i)\cup\{i\}) = 0$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.7: Using Properties to Simplify Computation

explanations.text: There are exactly $(n-1)!$ permutations where a fixed player $i$ is placed last.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.7: Using Properties to Simplify Computation
explanations.notes_quote: $(n-1)!$ such permutations out of $n!$

explanations.text: No player is a null player here: player $i$'s addition to coalition $N\setminus\{i\}$ changes the value from 0 to 1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Banzhaf index $\beta_i(v)$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\beta_i(v) = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \sum_{C \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} [v(C \cup \{i\}) - v(C)]$
The Banzhaf index averages marginal contributions over all coalitions not containing player $i$
The Banzhaf index averages over all $n!$ permutations of $N$
There are $2^{n-1}$ coalitions that do not contain player $i$
Like the Shapley value, the Banzhaf index measures expected marginal contributions but with a different averaging scheme

explanations.text: This is the exact formula for the Banzhaf index.
explanations.notes_quote: $\beta_i(v) = \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \sum_{C \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} [v(C \cup \{i\}) - v(C)]$

explanations.text: The Banzhaf index averages over all coalitions rather than all permutations.
explanations.notes_quote: the Banzhaf index measures agents' expected marginal contributions, but it averages over all coalitions rather than all permutations

explanations.text: Averaging over all $n!$ permutations is the Shapley value. The Banzhaf index sums over $2^{n-1}$ coalitions.
There are $2^{n-1}$ subsets of $N$ not containing player $i$.
explanations.notes_quote: The sum is over all $2^{n-1}$ coalitions that do not contain player $i$.

explanations.text: Both measure expected marginal contributions, but with different averaging: permutations vs. coalitions.
explanations.notes_quote: Like the Shapley value, the Banzhaf index measures agents' expected marginal contributions, but it averages over all coalitions rather than all permutations.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the properties satisfied by the Banzhaf index? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Banzhaf index satisfies the null player property
The Banzhaf index satisfies the symmetry property
The Banzhaf index satisfies the additivity property (for the unnormalized version)
The Banzhaf index satisfies efficiency: $\sum_{i} \beta_i(v) = v(N)$
The Banzhaf index satisfies three of the four Shapley axioms, failing only efficiency

explanations.text: The null player property is satisfied.
explanations.notes_quote: Null (Dummy) Player: Yes

explanations.text: The symmetry property is satisfied.
explanations.notes_quote: Symmetry: Yes

explanations.text: The additivity property is satisfied for the unnormalized Banzhaf index.
explanations.notes_quote: Additivity: Yes

explanations.text: Efficiency is the one Shapley axiom the Banzhaf index does NOT satisfy.
explanations.notes_quote: Efficiency: No -- the Banzhaf index does NOT necessarily distribute $v(N)$

explanations.text: The Banzhaf index satisfies null player, symmetry, and additivity, but fails efficiency.
explanations.notes_quote: The Banzhaf index satisfies three of the four Shapley value properties

questions.text: For the game with $n$ players where $v(N)=1$ and $v(C)=0$ for all $C \subsetneq N$, which of the following are correct about the Banzhaf index? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Player $i$'s marginal contribution is 1 only when $C = N \setminus \{i\}$, and 0 otherwise
$\sum_{i \in N} \beta_i = \frac{n}{2^{n-1}}$, which equals 1 for all $n \geq 2$
$\sum_{i \in N} \beta_i = \frac{n}{2^{n-1}} \neq 1$ for $n \geq 3$, confirming the lack of efficiency
For $n=2$, $\sum_{i} \beta_i = 1$, so the Banzhaf index is efficient in this case

explanations.text: Only the coalition $N\setminus\{i\}$ gives marginal contribution 1; all others give 0.
explanations.notes_quote: the marginal contribution is 1 only when $C = N \setminus \{i\}$, and 0 for all other coalitions

explanations.text: Since only one of $2^{n-1}$ coalitions gives contribution 1, $\beta_i = 1/2^{n-1}$.
$\sum \beta_i = n/2^{n-1}$, which equals 1 only when $n=2$. For $n\geq 3$ it does not equal 1.
explanations.notes_quote: $\sum_{i \in N} \beta_i = \frac{n}{2^{n-1}} \neq 1$ (for $n \geq 3$)

explanations.text: The sum $n/2^{n-1} \neq 1$ for $n\geq 3$, confirming the Banzhaf index fails efficiency.
explanations.notes_quote: Sum over all players: $\sum_{i \in N} \beta_i = \frac{n}{2^{n-1}} \neq 1$ (for $n \geq 3$), confirming the lack of efficiency.

explanations.text: For $n=2$: $2/2^1 = 1 = v(N)$, so efficiency holds in this special case.
explanations.notes_quote: $\sum_{i \in N} \beta_i = \frac{n}{2^{n-1}} \neq 1$ (for $n \geq 3$)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the normalized Banzhaf index and its trade-offs? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\beta_i^* = \frac{\beta_i}{\sum_{j \in N} \beta_j} \cdot v(N)$
Normalization restores the efficiency property
Normalization preserves the additivity property
There is a fundamental trade-off: normalization breaks additivity
The normalized Banzhaf index satisfies all four Shapley axioms

explanations.text: This is the exact formula for the normalized Banzhaf index.
explanations.notes_quote: $\beta_i^* = \frac{\beta_i}{\sum_{j \in N} \beta_j} \cdot v(N)$

explanations.text: Normalization is introduced specifically to ensure efficiency.
explanations.notes_quote: To ensure efficiency, we can normalize

explanations.text: Normalization breaks additivity — the notes state this is a fundamental trade-off.
explanations.notes_quote: normalization breaks the additivity property -- there is a fundamental trade-off

explanations.text: Normalization breaks additivity, creating a fundamental trade-off.
explanations.notes_quote: normalization breaks the additivity property -- there is a fundamental trade-off

explanations.text: Even after normalization the Banzhaf index fails additivity, so it cannot satisfy all four Shapley axioms.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7.6: Properties of the Shapley Value

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe power indices in simple games? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In simple games both the Shapley value and Banzhaf index have a natural interpretation as power indices
A swing player is one who turns a losing coalition into a winning one by joining it
In simple games, the Shapley value is called the Shapley-Shubik power index
Power indices measure the probability that a player is a veto player
Power indices measure the probability that a player can influence the outcome

explanations.text: In simple games both indices have a natural power index interpretation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games
explanations.notes_quote: both the Shapley value and Banzhaf index have a natural interpretation as power indices

explanations.text: A swing player turns a losing coalition into a winning one.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games
explanations.notes_quote: a swing player who turns a losing coalition into a winning one

explanations.text: In simple games the Shapley value is known as the Shapley-Shubik power index.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games
explanations.notes_quote: the Shapley value is called the Shapley-Shubik power index

explanations.text: Power indices measure the probability of being a swing player, not a veto player. These are distinct concepts.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games

explanations.text: Power indices measure the probability that a player can influence the outcome (i.e., is a swing player).
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.5: Power Indices in Simple Games
explanations.notes_quote: they measure the probability that a player can influence the outcome (i.e., is a swing player who turns a losing coalition into a winning one)

sections.title: 9. Summary and Comparison of Solution Concepts

questions.text: Which of the following correctly classify the three main solution concepts by type and key idea? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The core is a stability concept: no coalition can do better by deviating
The Shapley value is a fairness concept: average marginal contribution over all orderings
The Banzhaf index is a fairness concept: average marginal contribution over all coalitions
The Banzhaf index is a stability concept because it does not guarantee efficiency
The Shapley value is a stability concept because it always exists

explanations.text: The core is classified as a stability concept.
explanations.notes_quote: Core: Stability — No coalition can do better by deviating

explanations.text: The Shapley value is a fairness concept based on average marginal contributions over orderings.
explanations.notes_quote: Shapley value: Fairness — Average marginal contribution over all orderings

explanations.text: The Banzhaf index is a fairness concept: average marginal contribution over all coalitions.
explanations.notes_quote: Banzhaf index: Fairness — Average marginal contribution over all coalitions

explanations.text: The Banzhaf index is a fairness concept, not stability-based. Failing efficiency does not make it stability-based.
Always existing is a property of the Shapley value, but it remains a fairness concept, not stability-based.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly contrast the core and the Shapley value? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The core may be empty; the Shapley value always exists and is unique
The core is a set of outcomes; the Shapley value is a single payoff vector
The core guarantees stability but not necessarily fairness; the Shapley value guarantees fairness but not necessarily stability
Both the core (when non-empty) and the Shapley value are always efficient
The Shapley value guarantees stability; the core guarantees fairness

explanations.text: The core can be empty; the Shapley value always exists and is unique.
explanations.notes_quote: Always exists? Core: No (can be empty); Shapley Value: Yes (always unique)

explanations.text: The core is a set; the Shapley value is a single vector.
explanations.notes_quote: Can contain multiple outcomes? Core: Yes (a set); Shapley Value: No (a single vector)

explanations.text: The core guarantees stability but not fairness; the Shapley value guarantees fairness but not stability.
explanations.notes_quote: Guarantees stability? Core: Yes; Shapley Value: Not necessarily. Guarantees fairness? Core: Not necessarily; Shapley Value: Yes (by axioms)

explanations.text: Both are efficient: core when non-empty (by the stability-implies-efficiency theorem), and Shapley value always.
explanations.notes_quote: Efficient? Core: Yes (when non-empty); Shapley Value: Yes (always)

explanations.text: This reverses the classification. The core guarantees stability (not fairness), and the Shapley value guarantees fairness (not stability).

questions.source_ref: Section 9.3: Shapley Value vs. Banzhaf Index
questions.text: Which of the following correctly contrast the Shapley value and the Banzhaf index? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The Shapley value averages over $n!$ permutations; the Banzhaf index averages over $2^{n-1}$ coalitions
Both satisfy null player, symmetry, and additivity (for the unnormalized Banzhaf index)
The Shapley value satisfies efficiency; the Banzhaf index does not (unless normalized)
Normalizing the Banzhaf index restores efficiency while preserving additivity
The Shapley value is the unique scheme satisfying all four properties; no such uniqueness claim applies to the Banzhaf index

explanations.text: The comparison table confirms these different averaging denominators.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Shapley Value vs. Banzhaf Index
explanations.notes_quote: Averages over: Shapley Value — Permutations ($n!$); Banzhaf Index — Coalitions ($2^{n-1}$)

explanations.text: Both satisfy null player, symmetry, and additivity (for unnormalized Banzhaf).
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Shapley Value vs. Banzhaf Index
explanations.notes_quote: Null player: both Yes. Symmetry: both Yes. Additivity: Shapley Value Yes; Banzhaf Index Yes (but lost if normalized)

explanations.text: Efficiency holds for the Shapley value always, but not for the unnormalized Banzhaf index.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Shapley Value vs. Banzhaf Index
explanations.notes_quote: Efficiency: Shapley Value — Yes; Banzhaf Index — No (unless normalized)

explanations.text: Normalizing the Banzhaf index restores efficiency but breaks additivity — there is a fundamental trade-off.
explanations.notes_quote: normalization breaks the additivity property -- there is a fundamental trade-off

explanations.text: The Shapley value's uniqueness theorem has no direct analogue for the Banzhaf index.
explanations.section_ref: Section 9.3: Shapley Value vs. Banzhaf Index
explanations.notes_quote: Uniqueness: Shapley Value — Only scheme with all 4 properties; Banzhaf Index — --
